title.en: Michelson Interferometer - Part 1: Building & Basic Alignment

instruction.en:
Task 1: Align the laser beam along the optical axis.
The beam should pass through position [-2, -2] on the table where the beamsplitter will be placed.
Adjust the laser's rotation (x, y, z axes) to direct the beam correctly.

question.en: Where should the laser beam be directed?

answers.en: Through the center of the future beamsplitter position at [-2, -2]
Anywhere on the optical table
Directly at the screen position

instruction.en:
Task 2: Place the beamsplitter at 45° to the incoming laser beam.
The beamsplitter divides the light into two perpendicular paths.
First position it roughly at [-2, -2], then adjust the rotation so that both output beams are visible - one transmitted and one reflected.
Both beams should have approximately equal intensity.

question.en: What is the primary function of the beamsplitter in a Michelson interferometer?

answers.en: To divide the incoming beam into two perpendicular paths and recombine them
To amplify the laser light
To focus the beam onto the screen

instruction.en:
Task 3: Align Mirror 1 (the fixed mirror) to reflect the beam directly back along its incoming path.
This is called retroreflection. The beam should bounce back through the beamsplitter toward the laser.
Use small rotation adjustments around the x and y axes.
Watch the reflected beam - it should retrace its path.

question.en: When properly aligned as a retroreflector, what happens to the beam?

answers.en: It returns parallel to its incoming direction, back toward the source
It is scattered randomly in all directions
It disappears completely

instruction.en:
Task 4: Align Mirror 2 (the scanning mirror) in the second arm.
Both reflected beams should emerge from the beamsplitter parallel to each other.
Adjust the position (X, Y) and rotation (x, y) of Mirror 2 so that both beams travel together toward the future screen position.
The two beams should be at the same height and direction.

question.en: Why must both beams emerge parallel from the beamsplitter?

answers.en: Parallel beams can overlap perfectly and create stable interference patterns
It makes the setup look more symmetrical
Parallel beams travel faster

instruction.en:
Task 5: Insert the diverging lens into the laser beam.
The lens should be positioned between the laser and the beamsplitter.
Move the lens laterally (X, Y) until it intercepts the beam, then align its surfaces perpendicular to the beam.
The diverging lens will spread the beam, which is crucial for finding the exact zero path difference later.
You should see the beam spreading after passing through the lens.

question.en: Why do we use a diverging lens instead of parallel light for precise alignment?

answers.en: Divergent rays have different path lengths; only at zero OPD do ALL rays interfere constructively, making the zero-OPD position clearly visible
The lens makes the beam brighter
Divergent light has a longer wavelength

instruction.en:
Task 6: Both beams now reach the screen but appear as separate spots.
Your goal is to make them overlap perfectly.
Fine-tune the tip and tilt (rotations x and y) of Mirror 2 to merge the two spots into one.
When they overlap completely, interference fringes may become visible (in premium mode).
Imagine you are trying to superimpose two flashlight beams perfectly.

steps.comment: Solution hint: Mirror 2 at position [-4, -2], rotation [0, 0, 90]

instruction.en:
Task 7: Fine-tune the mirror angles to achieve circular interference fringes (Haidinger fringes).
Circular fringes indicate that the mirrors are exactly perpendicular to each other.
Each ring represents a contour of constant path difference.
The center marks the position of zero optical path difference.
Use very fine angular adjustments - even tiny changes matter!
In premium mode, you should see concentric rings when perfectly aligned.

question.en: What do circular (concentric) fringes tell you about the mirror alignment?

answers.en: The mirrors are exactly perpendicular, forming a perfect virtual air gap
One mirror is significantly tilted
The pathlengths are very different

instruction.en:
Task 8: Find the zero optical path difference (zero-OPD) position using divergent light.
Notice that Mirror 1 has been slightly offset. Now scan Mirror 2 along the Z-axis (toward and away from the beamsplitter).
With divergent light, interference fringes ONLY appear near the zero-OPD position.
Slowly move Mirror 2 while watching the screen for colored fringes to appear.
The position where fringes first become visible marks the exact equal-path condition.
This is a powerful technique used in precision metrology!

Hint: Start with Mirror 2 at Z = -3.0 and slowly increase to Z = -2.6.

question.en: Why does divergent light help you find the exact zero path difference?

answers.en: Different rays have different path lengths at most positions; only at zero OPD do ALL rays interfere constructively simultaneously, creating strong visible fringes
The lens amplifies the interference signal electronically
Divergent light has a longer coherence length

instruction.en:
Congratulations! You have successfully built and aligned a Michelson interferometer!

You learned:
• Step-by-step construction of the interferometer
• Retroreflection principle for mirror alignment
• How to overlap two beams for interference
• Using divergent light to find the exact zero path difference position
• The meaning of circular (Haidinger) fringes

This fundamental instrument won Albert Michelson the first Nobel Prize in Physics (1907) and is still used today in applications ranging from gravitational wave detection (LIGO) to optical coherence tomography (medical imaging).

Press DONE to proceed to the next level.
